description: Redis health, replication lag & role, memory utilisation, keyspace metrics, persistence status (AOF rewrite, BGSAVE latency), server-side command latency, evictions, per-instance breakdown bar gauges, and Top-N rankings for chrono-queue. High-level signals plus instant multi-instance comparison — see Redis Performance and Latency for detailed time-series and alert analysis.

panels.title: Redis Status
panels.description: Redis exporter availability across the selected Redis instances. Uses the minimum value so any selected down instance turns the stat red.

panels.title: Uptime
panels.description: Maximum uptime across the selected Redis instances. Useful for spotting recent Redis restarts.
Highest memory fragmentation ratio across the selected Redis instances. Values below 1 can indicate swapping, while sustained values above 1.5 suggest allocator fragmentation and wasted RSS.
panels.title: Memory Fragmentation Ratio

panels.description: Count of selected Redis instances reporting at least one connected replica. A value of 0 means no primary currently has a healthy replica attached.
panels.title: Replicas Healthy
Connected Clients
panels.description: Total connected Redis clients summed across the selected Redis instances.

panels.title: Blocked Clients
panels.description: Total blocked clients across the selected Redis instances. Sustained non-zero values usually indicate command stalls or backend contention.

Fleet cache hit rate: aggregate hits divided by total lookups (hits + misses) across all selected instances. Ratio is computed from combined counters — not an average of per-instance ratios — so high-traffic instances are correctly weighted. Values below 90% indicate a potential cache efficiency issue.
panels.title: Cache Hit Rate
Total Keys
panels.description: Total number of keys across all Redis databases. For chrono-queue this includes deduplication keys and retry metadata.
Aggregate eviction rate across the selected Redis instances. Any sustained non-zero eviction rate indicates memory pressure and should page an operator if the cache is expected to be lossless.
panels.title: Evictions/s

panels.description: Status of the last RDB background save. FAILED indicates data persistence is at risk.
panels.title: RDB Save Status
Memory Used
panels.description: Memory actively used by Redis for data storage. Does not include OS-allocated RSS overhead. Growing usage approaching maxmemory will trigger evictions.
Current used memory ranked per Redis instance. Replaces the opaque aggregate RSS stat to surface per-instance memory differences at a glance. See Memory Usage Over Time for trends.
panels.title: Memory Used per Instance

panels.description: Memory fragmentation ratio (RSS/Used) per Redis instance. >1.5 indicates fragmentation waste; <1.0 means Redis memory is being swapped to disk — critical risk. Replaces the single max() aggregate stat which masked per-instance divergence.
panels.title: Fragmentation Ratio per Instance

panels.description: Configured maxmemory limit. 0 means no limit is set (unlimited).
panels.title: Max Memory (configured)

panels.description: Aggregate Redis used memory across the selected instances over time. This isolates the total working-set trend from the more detailed per-instance memory breakdown.
panels.title: Redis Used Memory Trend

panels.description: Memory saturation view by Redis instance and application label. Comparing used, RSS, peak, and configured max memory makes memory pressure and fragmentation easier to spot before evictions begin.
panels.title: Memory Usage Over Time

panels.description: Memory pressure ratio per Redis instance and application. When maxmemory is configured (> 0): shows used/max ratio (true pressure). When maxmemory is unlimited (= 0): shows used/peak ratio as a proxy for memory headroom. Warn threshold at 70 % (yellow), critical at 85 % (red).
panels.title: Memory Pressure

targets.expr: (sum by (instance, service) (redis_memory_used_bytes{instance=~"$instance"} and on(instance, service) (redis_memory_max_bytes{instance=~"$instance"} > 0)) / clamp_min(sum by (instance, service) (redis_memory_max_bytes{instance=~"$instance"} and on(instance, service) (redis_memory_max_bytes{instance=~"$instance"} > 0)), 1e-9)) or (sum by (instance, service) (redis_memory_used_bytes{instance=~"$instance"}) / clamp_min(sum by (instance, service) (redis_memory_used_peak_bytes{instance=~"$instance"}), 1e-9))

panels.description: Key counts by Redis database with explicit instance/application labels for multi-instance or multi-tenant expansion.
panels.title: Keys by Database

panels.description: Separates memory-pressure evictions (red) from expected TTL expirations (blue) per instance. Red threshold line at > 0 evictions/s: any sustained eviction fires the alert. Alert PromQL: sum by (instance, service) (increase(redis_evicted_keys_total{instance=~"$instance"}[$__rate_interval])) > 0.
panels.title: Key Evictions & Expirations/s

panels.description: Unsaved write backlog by Redis instance/application label. Sustained growth usually means background saves are failing or not keeping pace with write volume.
panels.title: RDB Changes Since Last Save

panels.description: Total number of connected Redis replicas across the selected Redis instances. For a single primary this should align with your configured replica count.
panels.title: Connected Replicas

panels.description: Minimum replication link status over selected instances (1=UP, 0=DOWN). Any broken primary-replica link turns this panel red.
panels.title: Master Link Status

panels.description: Replication health trend by instance/application. Connected replicas (left axis) tracks fan-out; master link status highlights broken replication links. Role count series shows instance role distribution — step changes indicate failovers.
panels.title: Replication Health Over Time

targets.expr: (min by (instance, service) (redis_master_link_status{instance=~"$instance"}) or min by (instance, service) (redis_up{instance=~"$instance"}))

panels.description: Correlates Redis memory growth against eviction rate per instance. Right-axis threshold line at > 0 (red): any eviction fires the alert. Alert PromQL: sum by (instance, service) (increase(redis_evicted_keys_total{instance=~"$instance"}[$__rate_interval])) > 0. Rising memory with non-zero evictions indicates hard memory pressure and potential data churn.
panels.title: Correlation: Memory Used vs Evictions

panels.description: Correlates active client connections against rejected connection rate per instance. Right-axis threshold line at > 0 (red): any rejected connection fires the alert. Alert PromQL: sum by (instance, service) (increase(redis_rejected_connections_total{instance=~"$instance"}[$__rate_interval])) > 0. Rejected spikes near the maxclients ceiling indicate upstream pool exhaustion.
panels.title: Correlation: Connections vs Rejected Connections

panels.description: Correlates Redis CPU utilization (sys+user, % of one core) against command throughput by instance and application. A rising CPU-per-command ratio flags increasingly expensive workloads. Threshold lines at 70 % and 85 % match SLO alert levels.
panels.title: Correlation: CPU Utilization vs Command Rate

panels.description: Correlates aggregate Redis command throughput against mean Lettuce client latency per instance. Right-axis threshold lines: warn at 10 ms (yellow) and critical at 50 ms (red). A latency spike that tracks command rate growth is load-driven; divergence from command rate typically indicates a Redis-side regression (slowdown, blocking command, GC, swap).
panels.title: Correlation: Commands Rate vs Lettuce Avg Latency

targets.expr: 1000 * sum by (application, instance) (chrono:lettuce_command_completion_seconds_sum:rate5m{instance=~"$instance"}) / clamp_min(sum by (application, instance) (chrono:lettuce_command_completion_seconds_count:rate5m{instance=~"$instance"}), 1e-9)

panels.description: Ranked memory consumption per Redis instance. Surface highest-memory instances for targeted investigation before evictions begin.
panels.title: Memory Usage by Instance (Current)

panels.description: Current CPU utilisation per Redis instance. Use alongside the Correlation section to identify CPU-bound instances and correlate with command rate and latency.
panels.title: CPU % by Instance (Current)

panels.description: Current command throughput per Redis instance, Top-10 ranked. Reveals traffic imbalance across multi-instance deployments.
panels.title: Command Rate by Instance (Current)

panels.description: Top-10 Redis instances ranked by current memory consumption. Primary capacity planning signal for identifying eviction risk across a multi-instance deployment.
panels.title: Top-10 Memory Usage Instances

panels.description: Top-10 instances by total key count (all databases summed). High key cardinality correlates with memory growth and SCAN/KEYS latency.
panels.title: Top-10 Highest Key Count Instances

panels.description: Top-10 instances by current connected client count. Instances near maxclients risk rejecting new connections from upstream Spring Boot services.
panels.title: Top-10 Instances by Connected Clients

panels.description: Redis role per instance derived from redis_instance_info{role=...}. Expected values: ‘master’ (primary), ‘slave’ (replica), ‘sentinel’. Any instance unexpectedly showing ‘slave’ where a master is expected indicates a failover or misconfiguration. Use alongside Broken Replication Links and Replication Lag Analysis below.
panels.title: Redis Role per Instance

panels.description: Maximum replication lag in bytes across all selected replica instances, current snapshot. Warn at 1 MB (yellow), critical at 10 MB (red). Query: max(redis_master_repl_offset − redis_slave_repl_offset) per instance. See Replication Lag per Instance Over Time below for per-replica trends.
panels.title: Replication Lag (bytes, max across replicas)

targets.expr: max(max by (instance, service) (redis_master_repl_offset{instance=~"$instance"}) - on (instance, service) max by (instance, service) (redis_slave_repl_offset{instance=~"$instance"}))

panels.description: Maximum master replication offset across the selected instances — monotonically increasing counter of bytes written to the replication stream. Stale or non-increasing values on a primary indicate replication has stalled. See 'Replication Offset Advance Rate' for per-instance trend.
panels.title: Master Replication Offset

panels.description: Replication offset advance rate in bytes/s per instance. Master offset rate (solid) shows the write throughput entering the replication stream. Slave offset rate (dashed) should closely track master rate when healthy. A divergence between master and slave advance rates will manifest as growing lag in the panels below.
panels.title: Replication Offset Advance Rate (bytes/s)

panels.description: Whether an AOF rewrite (BGREWRITEAOF) is currently in progress per instance. Sustained in-progress rewrites indicate the AOF file is large or I/O is slow, and can cause write amplification. Alert reference: redis_aof_rewrite_in_progress{instance=~"$instance"} == 1
panels.title: AOF Rewrite In Progress

panels.description: Duration of the last completed AOF background rewrite in seconds. Values consistently above 10 s indicate slow disk I/O or an oversized AOF file — consider tuning auto-aof-rewrite-percentage and auto-aof-rewrite-min-size.
panels.title: AOF Last Rewrite Duration

panels.description: Duration of the last completed RDB background save (BGSAVE) in seconds. Elevated values indicate large dataset or disk I/O contention. A value of -1 means BGSAVE has never completed successfully.
panels.title: RDB Last BGSAVE Duration

panels.description: Current AOF file size in bytes. Rapid unbounded growth without periodic rewrite indicates the auto-rewrite thresholds may be misconfigured.
panels.title: AOF Last COW Size

panels.description: AOF rewrite in-progress flag and persistence operation durations over time by instance/application. Correlate AOF rewrite spikes with key evictions and latency increases to identify write-amplification headroom issues.
panels.title: Persistence Latency Over Time (AOF Rewrite & RDB BGSAVE)

panels.description: Average server-side execution latency per Redis command derived from redis_commands_duration_seconds_total / redis_commands_total counters (available when latency-tracking is enabled or via redis_exporter ≥1.44). Use this to identify expensive commands (e.g., LRANGE, SMEMBERS, KEYS) contributing to tail latency observed by chrono-queue workers. Falls back to zero if the metric is not present.
panels.title: Avg Server-Side Command Latency by Command

panels.description: Top-10 Redis commands ranked by call rate (ops/s) over the last 5 minutes. High-frequency commands with elevated avg latency from the panel above are the primary tuning targets.
panels.title: Top-10 Commands by Call Rate

panels.description: Top-10 Redis commands ranked by total CPU duration seconds/s (rate of redis_commands_duration_seconds_total). Commands near the top of this list are the primary contributors to Redis CPU saturation regardless of call rate.
panels.title: Top-10 Commands by CPU Duration (s/s)

panels.description: Top-10 Redis instances ranked by current key eviction rate (keys/s). Any instance with sustained non-zero evictions is under hard memory pressure and actively discarding data. Cross-reference with 'Top-10 Memory Usage Instances' and 'Memory Pressure' to find the root cause: either maxmemory is too low or the working set has grown unexpectedly.
panels.title: Top-10 Highest Eviction Rate Instances

panels.description: Top-10 Redis instances ranked by rejected connection rate (connections/s). Rejected connections indicate the instance is at or over its maxclients limit. Any non-zero value in production is immediately actionable: either raise maxclients, add pool-side backpressure, or provision additional Redis capacity.
panels.title: Top-10 Highest Rejected Connection Rate

panels.description: Top-10 Redis instances ranked by slowlog queue depth. Complements the 'Avg Server-Side Command Latency' time-series. A growing slowlog depth on a specific instance while others remain clean isolates the problem to that instance's workload or configuration rather than a fleet-wide regression.
panels.title: Top-10 Highest Slowlog Depth Instances

panels.description: Firing instance count. Alert PromQL: min by (instance, service) (redis_up{instance=~"$instance"}) == 0
panels.title: Alert: Redis Down Instances

targets.expr: sum((min by (instance, service) (redis_up{instance=~"$instance"}) == bool 0) or vector(0))

panels.description: Firing instance count. Alert PromQL: sum by (instance, service) (increase(redis_evicted_keys_total{instance=~"$instance"}[$__rate_interval])) > 0
panels.title: Alert: Key Evictions

panels.description: Firing instance count. Alert PromQL: sum by (instance, service) (increase(redis_rejected_connections_total{instance=~"$instance"}[$__rate_interval])) > 0
panels.title: Alert: Rejected Connections

panels.description: Firing instance count. Alert PromQL: 100 * sum by (instance, service) (rate(redis_cpu_sys_seconds_total{instance=~"$instance"}[$__rate_interval]) + rate(redis_cpu_user_seconds_total{instance=~"$instance"}[$__rate_interval])) > 80
panels.title: Alert: CPU Above 80%

panels.description: Firing instance count. Alert PromQL: max by (instance, service) (redis_slowlog_length{instance=~"$instance"}) > 0
panels.title: Alert: Slowlog Activity

targets.expr: sum((max by (instance, service) (redis_slowlog_length{instance=~"$instance"}) > bool 0) or vector(0))

panels.description: Count of application × instance combinations where p95 Lettuce latency exceeds the 100 ms SLO threshold. Alert PromQL: histogram_quantile(0.95, sum by (le, application, instance, command) (chrono:lettuce_command_completion_seconds_bucket:increase5m{instance=~"$instance"})) > 0.100
panels.title: Alert: p95 Latency > 100 ms

targets.expr: sum(  max by (application, instance) (    chrono:lettuce_command_completion_p95_seconds:5m{instance=~"$instance"}  ) > bool 0.100 or vector(0))

panels.description: Count of Redis instances operating as primary (master) across the selection. A master count of 0 means no primary is reachable — which is a full-service outage signal.
panels.title: Master Instances

panels.description: Count of Redis instances currently operating as replicas (slaves) across the selection. Use alongside Master Instances to verify the expected topology (e.g., 1 master, 2 replicas).
panels.title: Replica Instances

panels.description: Number of Redis instances with master link DOWN (redis_master_link_status == 0). Any non-zero value indicates broken replication: the replica is not receiving updates from its master. Alert PromQL: min by (instance, service) (redis_master_link_status{instance=~"$instance"}) == 0
panels.title: Broken Replication Links

targets.expr: sum((min by (instance, service) (redis_master_link_status{instance=~"$instance"}) == bool 0) or vector(0))

panels.description: Maximum replication lag in bytes across all replica instances. Computed as redis_master_repl_offset − redis_slave_repl_offset on each slave instance. Warn at 1 MB (yellow), critical at 10 MB (red). Sustained lag under normal write rate indicates a stalled or slow replica.
panels.title: Max Replication Lag (bytes)

targets.expr: max(clamp_min(  max by (instance, service) (redis_master_repl_offset{instance=~"$instance"})  - max by (instance, service) (redis_slave_repl_offset{instance=~"$instance"}),  0) or vector(0))

panels.description: Per-replica replication lag in bytes over time, computed as redis_master_repl_offset − redis_slave_repl_offset per slave instance. Panel is empty for standalone Redis with no replicas (correct — there is no lag to measure). SLO threshold lines: warn at 1 MB (1048576 bytes, yellow), critical at 10 MB (10485760, red). A growing lag without a corresponding increase in write throughput indicates a struggling replica or network degradation between master and replica.
panels.title: Replication Lag per Instance Over Time (bytes)

targets.expr: clamp_min(max by (instance, service) (redis_master_repl_offset{instance=~"$instance"}) - max by (instance, service) (redis_slave_repl_offset{instance=~"$instance"}), 0)
(min by (instance, service) (redis_master_link_status{instance=~"$instance"}) or min by (instance, service) (redis_up{instance=~"$instance"}))

panels.description: Instance count per Redis role (master / slave / sentinel) over time. Step changes indicate role transitions: a failover shows as master count dropping by 1 then recovering, while slave count may briefly drop 1 then rise. Use this alongside the Broken Replication Links stat to distinguish planned failovers from failures. Labeled and color-coded by role label from redis_instance_info.
panels.title: Role Distribution Over Time

panels.description: Ranked replication lag per slave instance, current snapshot. Surfaces the most-lagging replica instantly. Warn threshold at 1 MB (yellow), critical at 10 MB (red). Only slave instances compute a non-zero lag; master instances emit 0 and will rank last.
panels.title: Top-10 Instances by Replication Lag (bytes)

targets.expr: topk(10, clamp_min(  max by (instance, service) (redis_master_repl_offset{instance=~"$instance"})  - max by (instance, service) (redis_slave_repl_offset{instance=~"$instance"}),  0))

panels.description: Per-instance replication link status (1=UP, 0=DOWN) over time. Each series is labeled with instance and role from redis_instance_info. Any series that drops to 0 indicates that replica lost its connection to the master. A simultaneous drop across all replicas may indicate master unavailability.
panels.title: Replica Link Status per Instance

targets.expr: (min by (instance, service) (redis_master_link_status{instance=~"$instance"}) or min by (instance, service) (redis_up{instance=~"$instance"}))

panels.description: Absolute replication offsets for master instances (solid green) and slave instances (dashed blue). A widening gap between master and any slave line confirms lag accumulation. For standalone Redis with no replicas only the Primary offset line is shown; Replica lines appear when redis_slave_repl_offset is reported. Primary labeled 'Primary (master)', replicas labeled 'Replica (slave)'.
panels.title: Master vs Replica Offset Divergence

targets.expr: max by (instance, service) (redis_master_repl_offset{instance=~"$instance"} * on(instance, service) group_left(role) label_replace(redis_instance_info{role="master", instance=~"$instance"}, "dummy", "", "", ""))

title: Redis — Health & Keyspace
